sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: MOUSE(row=33, col=8).
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 237, level 3.
Valid actions right now: MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 3: you have used 182 actions; a strong score needs about 26 or fewer. You are 7.0x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content: Let me check what changed after clicking on the Left H button at (33, 8).

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check what changed
if previous_frame:
    prev_lines = previous_frame.ascii.split('\n')
    curr_lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
    
    changed_rows = []
    for r in range(len(prev_lines)):
        if prev_lines[r] != curr_lines[r]:
            changed_rows.append(r)
    
    print(f"Changed rows: {changed_rows}")
    for r in changed_rows:
        if r != 1:  # Skip HUD row
            print(f"  Row {r}: '{curr_lines[r]}'")
            for c in range(len(prev_lines[r])):
                if prev_lines[r][c] != curr_lines[r][c]:
                    print(f"    Col {c}: '{prev_lines[r][c]}' -> '{curr_lines[r][c]}'")

# Check T-shape position
lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
t_shape_rows = []
for r in range(7, 31):
    row_str = lines[r][6:31]
    if 'c' in row_str:
        c_positions = [i+6 for i, c in enumerate(row_str) if c == 'c']
        t_shape_rows.append((r, c_positions))
print(f"\nT-shape positions: {t_shape_rows}")

print(f"\nLast action: gameplay_changed={last_action_result.get('gameplay_changed')}, no_impact={last_action_result.get('no_impact')}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python
sections.content:
Changed rows: [1]

T-shape positions: [(16, [22, 25]), (17, [22, 23, 24, 25]), (18, [22, 23, 24, 25]), (19, [22, 23, 24, 25])]

Last action: gameplay_changed=False, no_impact=True

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Clicking on (33, 8) didn't change the gameplay - only the HUD changed. The T-shape is still at the same position. So clicking on the Left H button at (33, 8) is a no-impact action.
Wait, but earlier clicking on (33, 34) did change the gameplay. Let me check what's different.
Looking at the Left H buttons: 'gwwwggwwwggwwwggwwwggggggB'
The 'w' cells are at cols 7-9, 11-13, 15-17, 19-21. I clicked on col 8, which is a 'w' cell. But it didn't change the gameplay.
Hmm, maybe the Left H buttons are not toggleable, or maybe they work differently from the Right H buttons.
Let me try clicking on a different Left H button position, or maybe the V buttons.
Actually, wait. Looking at the segmentation data, the Left H buttons are at row 33, and they're 'w' (light gray) cells. The Right H buttons are also at row 33, and they're 'w' cells too. But clicking on the Left H button didn't change anything, while clicking on the Right H button did.
Maybe the Left H buttons are not interactive, or maybe they're already in the correct state and can't be toggled.
Let me try clicking on the Right V buttons to see if they're toggleable.
Actually, let me step back and reconsider the game mechanics. The fact that clicking on the Right H button at (33, 34) changed the button state from 'www' to 'BBB' suggests that the Right H buttons are toggleable. But clicking on the Left H button at (33, 8) didn't change anything.
Maybe the Left H buttons are not interactive, and only the Right H and V buttons are.
Let me try clicking on a Right V button to see if it's toggleable.
Looking at the Right V buttons: 'WwWWWWwWWWWwWWWWwWWWWwWWWWwW'
The 'w' cells are at cols 34, 38, 42, 46, 50, 54, 58. Let me try clicking on one of them.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Let me try clicking on a Right V button
# Right V buttons are at row 35, cols 33-60
# 'WwWWWWwWWWWwWWWWwWWWWwWWWWwW'
# The 'w' cells are at cols 34, 38, 42, 46, 50, 54, 58

# First, let me check the current state of the Right H buttons (after the earlier toggle)
lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
right_h = lines[33][33:61]
print(f"Right H buttons: '{right_h}'")

# Try clicking on a Right V button at (35, 34)
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 35, 'col': 34}])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
